learning_outcomes:
1 1.  |  Major
Programme:
I.  |  Chemistry
A.  |  Knowledge Outcomes
Graduates should possess fundamental knowledge of the
following:
(1)  |  chemistry principles, concepts,
and applications of inorganic chemistry, organic chemistry, physical
chemistry, and analytical chemistry; and
(2)  |  mathematical techniques required
in chemical science.
Graduates should have the ability
of applying the acquired knowledge in exploring chemistry-related problems.
B.  |  Skills Outcomes
Professional Skills
Graduates should have a good
mastery of professional skills, including the following:
(1)  |  ability of recognizing key factors
and applying appropriate assumption to formulate solutions for chemistry problems;
(2)  |  basic mathematical skills for
handling experimental data and quantitative arguments in physical chemistry;
(3)  |  ability of determining molecular
structure based on spectroscopic measurements;
(4)  |  ability to implement safety
measures in conducting chemical laboratory works;
(5)  |  ability to operate instruments in
chemistry laboratories;
(6)  |  ability in carrying out prescribed
procedures using suited experimental skills and instrumentation;
(7)  |  ability to conduct literature
reviews/experimental works and to report scientific findings in both oral and
written formats; and
(8)  |  competence in using commercial
computer software for graphical depiction of molecular systems.
Graduates should be able to investigate
chemistry disciplines using combinations of the above skills.
Generic Skills
Graduates should have
acquired the following generic skills:
(1)  |  problem solving skills including
ability to identify key issues and formulate feasible solutions of problems
based on logical analysis and critical thinking;
(2)  |  ability to sort out complex
observations to construct logical proposition;
(3)  |  ability of formulating logical
sequence in arguments;
(4)  |  critical evaluation of issues
based on evidence and scientific logic;
(5)  |  ability of analyzing issues
quantitatively with the sense of level of uncertainty and reliability;
(6)  |  ability to acquire new knowledge
by self-study and to conduct independent work with logical planning;
(7)  |  interpersonal skills including the
ability to work effectively in a group;
(8)  |  information retrieval skills
through library and internet; and
(9)  |  time management skills.
C.  |  Attitude/Value Outcomes
Graduates should have developed the following
attitudes and values:
(1)  |  appreciation of the beauty of
chemistry and its importance as a central science;
(2)  |  curiosity and interest in
scientific exploration;
(3)  |  awareness of impacts of chemistry
in environmental, social, economical, industrial, and technological aspects;
(4)  |  awareness of chemical safety;
(5)  |  confidence in ability of solving
problems;
(6)  |  motivation and ability for
life-long learning; and
(7)  |  sense of taking responsibility in
study and work environments.
II.  |  Enrichment Stream
A.  |  Knowledge Outcomes
Graduates should possess fundamental knowledge of the
following:
(1)  |  chemistry principles, concepts,
and applications of inorganic chemistry, organic chemistry, physical
chemistry, and analytical chemistry;
(2)  |  in-depth understanding of disciplines
in chemistry to establish an integral picture of chemical science; and
(3)  |  mathematical techniques required
in chemical science.
With a solid background in
chemistry, graduates should have the ability of applying the acquired
knowledge in studying chemistry-related problems as well as pursuing a
research-oriented career.
B.  |  Skills Outcomes
Professional Skills
Graduates should have a good
mastery of professional skills, including the following:
(1)  |  ability of recognizing key factors
and applying appropriate assumption to formulate solutions for chemistry
problems;
(2)  |  awareness of recent advances in
chemical sciences;
(3)  |  basic mathematical skills for
handling experimental data and quantitative arguments in physical chemistry;
(4)  |  ability of determining molecular
structure based on spectroscopic measurements;
(5)  |  ability to implement safety
measures in conducting chemical laboratory works;
(6)  |  ability to operate instruments in
chemistry laboratories;
(7)  |  ability in carrying out prescribed
procedures using suited experimental skills and instrumentation;
(8)  |  ability to conduct literature
reviews/experimental works and to report scientific findings in both oral and
written formats; and
(9)  |  competence in using commercial
computer software for graphical depiction of molecular systems.
Graduates should be able to pursue
a research-oriented career in chemistry-related disciplines using
combinations of the above skills.
Generic Skills
Graduates should have
acquired the following generic skills:
(1)  |  problem solving skills including
ability to identify key issues and formulate feasible solutions of problems
based on logical analysis and critical thinking;
(2)  |  ability to sort out complex
observations to construct logical proposition;
(3)  |  ability of formulating logical
sequence in arguments;
(4)  |  critical evaluation of issues
based on evidence and scientific logic;
(5)  |  ability of analyzing issues
quantitatively with the sense of level of uncertainty and reliability;
(6)  |  ability to acquire new knowledge
by self-study and to conduct independent work with logical planning;
(7)  |  interpersonal skills including the
ability to work effectively in a group;
(8)  |  information retrieval skills
through library and internet; and
(9)  |  time management skills.
C.  |  Attitude/Value Outcomes
Graduates should have developed the following
attitudes and values:
(1)  |  appreciation of the beauty of
chemistry and its importance as a central science;
(2)  |  curiosity and interest in
scientific exploration;
(3)  |  awareness of impacts of chemistry
in environmental, social, economical, industrial, and technological aspects;
(4)  |  awareness of chemical safety;
(5)  |  confidence in ability of solving
problems;
(6)  |  motivation and ability for
life-long learning; and
(7)  |  sense of taking responsibility in
study and work environments.
III.  |  Testing and Accreditation
Stream
A.  |  Knowledge Outcomes
Graduates should possess fundamental knowledge of the
following:
(1)  |  chemistry principles, concepts, and
applications of inorganic chemistry, organic chemistry, physical chemistry,
and analytical chemistry;
(2)  |  in-depth understanding of
analytical chemistry and its application in chemical testing; and
(3)  |  mathematical techniques required
in chemical science.
Graduates should have the ability
of applying the acquired knowledge to solve problems in accredited chemical
testing.
B.  |  Skills Outcomes
Professional Skills
Graduates should have a good
mastery of professional skills, including the following:
(1)  |  ability of recognizing key factors
and applying appropriate assumption to formulate solutions for chemistry
problems;
(2)  |  basic mathematical skills for
handling experimental data and quantitative arguments in physical chemistry;
(3)  |  awareness of recent developments
in testing industry;
(4)  |  ability of applying spectroscopic
measurements for chemical testing;
(5)  |  ability to implement safety
measures in conducting chemical laboratory work;
(6)  |  ability to operate instruments in
chemistry laboratories;
(7)  |  ability in carrying out prescribed
procedures using suited experimental skills and instrumentation;
(8)  |  ability to conduct literature
reviews/experimental works and to report scientific findings in both oral and
written formats; and
(9)  |  competence in using commercial
computer software for graphical depiction of molecular systems.
Graduates should be able to
perform accredited chemical tests and resolve related issues using
combinations of the above skills.
Generic Skills
Graduates should have
acquired the following generic skills:
(1)  |  problem solving skills including
ability to identify key issues and formulate feasible solutions of problems
based on logical analysis and critical thinking;
(2)  |  ability to sort out complex
observations to construct logical proposition;
(3)  |  ability of formulating logical
sequence in arguments;
(4)  |  critical evaluation of issues
based on evidence and scientific logic;
(5)  |  ability of analyzing issues
quantitatively with the sense of level of uncertainty and reliability;
(6)  |  ability to acquire new knowledge
by self-study and to conduct independent work with logical planning;
(7)  |  interpersonal skills including the
ability to work effectively in a group;
(8)  |  information retrieval skills through
library and internet; and
(9)  |  time management skills.
C.  |  Attitude/Value Outcomes
Graduates should have developed the following
attitudes and values:
(1)  |  appreciation of the beauty of
chemistry and its importance as a central science;
(2)  |  curiosity and interest in
scientific exploration;
(3)  |  awareness of impacts of chemistry
in environmental, social, economical, industrial, and technological aspects;
(4)  |  awareness of chemical safety;
(5)  |  confidence in ability of solving
problems;
(6)  |  motivation and ability for
life-long learning; and
(7)  |  sense of taking responsibility in
study and work environments.
2.  |  Chemistry and Biology Double Major Programme:
Upon completion
of the programme, students will have acquired the abilities as follows:
Knowledge outcomes To train our students to have fundamental and advanced knowledge
of modern biological sciences and the ability of applying the acquired
knowledge in exploring chemistry-related problems. Specifically, they
include:
(1)  |  Basic
knowledge in biology, including diversity and functioning of various living
organisms
(2)  |  In‐depth
understanding in at least one major area of biology, such as physiology,
ecology and evolution, and human biology
(3)  |  Chemistry
principles, concepts, and applications of inorganic chemistry, organic
chemistry, physical chemistry, and analytical chemistry
(4)  |  Mathematical
techniques required in chemical science
Skills
outcomes With
our team of teaching staff coming from different professional backgrounds and
with teaching styles, students will be exposed to various learning activities
and challenges throughout their 4 years of study, during which they can
develop professional skills and generic attributes including active
learning, self-learning, effective communication, critical thinking, team
playing, as well as analytical and problem-solving skills. Specifically, they include:
(1)  |  Basic
laboratory/field techniques in at least one major line of modern biological
research, including molecular biology, physiology and field biology
(2)  |  Ability
of recognizing key factors and applying appropriate assumption to formulate
solutions for chemistry problems
(3)  |  Basic
mathematical skills for handling experimental data and quantitative arguments
in physical chemistry
(4)  |  Ability
of determining molecular structure based on spectroscopic measurements
(5)  |  Ability
to implement safety measures in conducting chemical laboratory works
(6)  |  Ability
to operate instruments in chemistry laboratories
(7)  |  Ability
in carrying out prescribed procedures using suited experimental skills and
instrumentation
(8)  |  Ability
to conduct literature reviews/experimental works and to report scientific findings
in both oral and written formats
(9)  |  Competence
in using commercial computer software for graphical depiction of molecular
systems
(10)  |  Problem
solving skills including ability to identify key issues and formulate
feasible solutions of problems based on logical analysis and critical
thinking
(11)  |  Ability
to sort out complex observations to construct logical proposition
(12)  |  Ability
of formulating logical sequence in arguments
(13)  |  Critical
evaluation of issues based on evidence and scientific logic
(14)  |  Ability
of analysing issues quantitatively with the sense of level of uncertainty and
reliability
(15)  |  Ability
to acquire new knowledge by self-study and to conduct independent work with
logical planning
(16)  |  Interpersonal
skills including the ability to work effectively in a group
(17)  |  Information
retrieval skills through library and internet
(18)  |  Time
management skills
Values and attitude outcomes V
= We
also concern about the well-being of our students. Apart from the
long-established academic advisory system, there are ample chances for
teachers and students to meet and exchange ideas formally and informally.
These activities, organised by the programme, Biology Society, Chemistry
Society or Alumni Association of the Department of Biology, The Chinese
University of Hong Kong (CUBIOAA), include tea parties, dinners, BBQ, and
career talks. From these, we believe our students are primed with positive values,
and will be open and respectful to different views or ideas, so as to act as
positive contributors in our society, no matter what fields and positions
they are in. Our students also have the ability to appreciate the nature, and
diversity of life and thus, would contributing to preserving our mother
Earth. Specifically, they include:
(1)  |  Appreciation
of biological knowledge and the beauty of chemistry and their importance as
central science
(2)  |  Sense
of biological conservation and other environmental issues
(3)  |  Awareness
on the latest developments and advancements in biology
(4)  |  Awareness
of impacts of chemistry in environmental, social, economic, industrial, and
technological aspects
(5)  |  Awareness
of chemical safety
(6)  |  Curiosity
and interest in scientific exploration
(7)  |  Confidence
in ability of solving problems
(8)  |  Motivation
and ability for life-long learning
(9)  |  Sense
of taking responsibility in study and work environments
3.  |  Minor
Programme:
Upon the completion of the
Chemistry Minor Programme, students will have demonstrated basic
understanding of fundamental issues related to Chemistry such as Analytical
Chemistry, Inorganic Chemistry, Organic Chemistry and Physical Chemistry.
They will have acquired knowledge of some aspects of the subjects with
sufficient exposure and at a suitable level to allow further study in other
areas in Science. While most skills and
attitudes expected of a graduate would have been developed through the Major
Programme, graduates who have been awarded a Minor will have developed the
basic technical skills and attitudes towards issues in Chemistry. They will
also be aware of the latest developments in Chemistry, and the impact of
Chemistry in social, industrial, environmental and technological contexts.

Science, Technology And
Research Stream
Students are required to complete
a minimum of 12 units of courses as follows:
Units
1.  |  Required
Courses:
(a)  |  One Faculty Package Course:  |  3
Choose from the two remaining groups of the Faculty
Package that have not been used to fulfill the Faculty Package
Requirement
(b)  |  Research Courses:  |  6
STAR2000, 3000, 4000[a]
(c)  |  Seminar Courses:  |  3

exposure[b]

Explanatory Notes:
1.  |  CHEM courses at 2000 and above level as well as those
labeled as # will be included in the calculation of Major GPA for honours
classification.
2.  |  Potential students majoring in Chemistry are strongly
recommended to take CHEM1870 as basic training to prepare for laboratory
classes in upper years.
3.  |  A student in the final year of attendance may, under
special circumstances and with written approval from the Department,
select CHEM4480 and/or 4490 to substitute up to two units of any lecture
or laboratory courses in the Chemistry Programme.
4.  |  Students in their final year of attendance may,
subject to approval by the Department, select CHEM4980/4990 and submit an
undergraduate thesis in place of CHEM4030/4040.
[a]  |  Students may select research-oriented course(s), as
approved by the Major Programme, to substitute up to 4 units for
fulfillment of Research Courses requirement.
[b]  |  Students must complete any
exchange/research/internship programme(s) offered by the University,
Colleges, the Faculty of Science or Major Programme, as approved by the
Major Programme, to fulfill the Experiential Learning requirement.
Students are responsible for the extra costs incurred in the exchange/research/internship
programme(s).
